coredata.dc:title: Intelligent quantitative recognition of sulfide using machine learning-based ratiometric fluorescence probe of metal-organic framework UiO-66-NH2/Ppix
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Degui
Yu, Long
Li, Xin
Lu, Yunfei
Niu, Chaoqun
Fan, Penghui
Zhu, Houjuan
Chen, Bing
Wang, Suhua
coredata.dc:description: Sulfides possess either high toxicity or play crucial physiological role such as gas transmitter dependent upon dosage, hence the significant for their rapid sensitive and selective concentration determination. Herein, a machine learning enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensor was engineered for sulfide determination by incorporating the nanometal-organic framework (UiO-66-NH2) along with protoporphyrin IX (Ppix). The blue fluorescence at 431 nm originated from the moiety of UiO-66-NH2 by 365 nm excitation serves as an internal calibration reference signal, while the red fluorescence at 629 nm from the moiety of Ppix serves as the analytical signal, and the intensity is correlated to the amount of sulfides. The fluorescence color of the sensor gradually varies from blue to red upon sequential addition of copper and sulfide ions, resulting in RGB (Red, Green, Blue) feature values for corresponding sulfide concentrations, which facilities the advanced data processing techniques using machine learning algorithms. On the basis of fluorescence image fingerprint extraction and machine learning algorithms, an online data analysis model was developed to improve the precision and accuracy of sulfide determination. The established model employed Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) and was subjected to rigorous cross-validation to ensure its robustness. By analyzing the correlation between RGB feature values and sulfide concentrations, the study highlighted a significant positive relationship between the red feature values and sulfide concentrations. The application of machine learning techniques on the ratiometric fluorescence signal of the UiO-66-NH2/Ppix probe demonstrated its potential for intelligent quantitative determination of sulfides, offering a valuable and efficient tool for pollution detection and real-time rapid environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfides
Uio-66-NH2
Protoporphyrin IX
Ratiometric fluorescence
Machine learning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence imaging and feature data extraction
Sensing mechanism based on ratiometric fluorescence
Tandem detection of Cu2+ and S2- based on fluorometer

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U21A20290 , 82001957 , 22176044 ), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2022A1515011656 ), the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT ( 2023rcyj1003 ), and Maoming Science and Technology Project ( 2023382 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FE-SEM image of Uio-66-NH2. (b and c) TEM images of Uio-66-NH2. Inset: particle size distribution of synthetic products. (d) EDS spectra. Inset: normalized mass distribution. (e) XPS spectra. (f) High-resolution XPS spectra of Zr 3d of Uio-66-NH2. (g) XRD spectra. Inset: picture of products. (h) FT-IR spectra. (i) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of Uio-66-NH2 in PBS buffer (pH=7). Inset: picture of UV lamp illumination (λex=365 nm) under darkness.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of five samples (ex=365 nm). Inset: fluorescence images with 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Normalized absorption and fluorescence spectra of Uio-66-NH2 and Ppix solution. (c) The fluorescence spectra of the FRET system with different concentrations of Ppix. (d) Color coordinates of Uio-66-NH2/Ppix, Uio-66-NH2/Ppix/Cu2+, and Uio-66-NH2/Ppix/Cu2+/S2- in CIE color space. (e) Absorption spectra of five samples. Inset: photographs of five samples in natural light. (f) XRD spectra.

(a) Luminescence spectra of Uio-66-NH2/Ppix at different Cu2+ concentrations (0.1–10 μM). (b) Non-linear curve of fluorescence intensity ratio (F629/F431) versus Cu2+ concentration. (c) Luminescence spectra of probe solution (Uio-66-NH2/Ppix/Cu2+) when adding different S2- concentrations (0–90 μM). [Cu2+] = 5 μM. (d) Variation of F629/F431 with different S2- concentrations (10–500 μM). (e) Probe selectivity experiments on S2-. (f) Probe anti-interference experiments on S2-. Blue prisms show the addition of various interfering substances (0.5 M) to the probe, and red prisms show the subsequent addition of 100 μM of S2-.
Distribution Statistics of Sample Fluorescence Feature Data. (a) Violin plot of RGB feature data distribution. (b) Radial bar chart of original data. (c) Radial bar chart of standardized data.

LDA Model Training and Prediction Results. (a) Classification discriminant plot of the LDA model for the five-fold training set. (b) Confusion matrix plot of the LDA model for both the training and testing sets, where the horizontal axis represents predicted categories and the vertical axis denotes actual categories. (c) Bar chart illustrating the classification accuracy of the LDA model through five-fold cross-validation.
Multiple linear regression analysis diagram. (a) variable correlation coefficient and correlation heat map. (b) Red characteristics and concentration linear regression analysis diagram.
(A) Fluorescent probe response mechanism. (B) Machine learning-based online analysis model.

head.ce:title: Intelligent quantitative recognition of sulfide using machine learning-based ratiometric fluorescence probe of metal-organic framework UiO-66-NH2/Ppix

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Energy, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Energy, South China University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Energy, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, A⁎STAR (Agency for Science, Technology and Research), 138634, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, A⁎STAR (Agency for Science, Technology and Research)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, A⁎STAR (Agency for Science, Technology and Research), Singapore 138634, Singapore

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfides possess either high toxicity or play crucial physiological role such as gas transmitter dependent upon dosage, hence the significant for their rapid sensitive and selective concentration determination. Herein, a machine learning enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensor was engineered for sulfide determination by incorporating the nanometal-organic framework (UiO-66-NH2) along with protoporphyrin IX (Ppix). The blue fluorescence at 431 nm originated from the moiety of UiO-66-NH2 by 365 nm excitation serves as an internal calibration reference signal, while the red fluorescence at 629 nm from the moiety of Ppix serves as the analytical signal, and the intensity is correlated to the amount of sulfides. The fluorescence color of the sensor gradually varies from blue to red upon sequential addition of copper and sulfide ions, resulting in RGB (Red, Green, Blue) feature values for corresponding sulfide concentrations, which facilities the advanced data processing techniques using machine learning algorithms. On the basis of fluorescence image fingerprint extraction and machine learning algorithms, an online data analysis model was developed to improve the precision and accuracy of sulfide determination. The established model employed Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) and was subjected to rigorous cross-validation to ensure its robustness. By analyzing the correlation between RGB feature values and sulfide concentrations, the study highlighted a significant positive relationship between the red feature values and sulfide concentrations. The application of machine learning techniques on the ratiometric fluorescence signal of the UiO-66-NH2/Ppix probe demonstrated its potential for intelligent quantitative determination of sulfides, offering a valuable and efficient tool for pollution detection and real-time rapid environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfides, including S2-, HS-, and H2S, are pervasive pollutants in wastewater and surface water. They arise from industrial processes and biological metabolism, and their excessive accumulation can cause severe environmental and biological hazards [1–3]. Protonated HS- and H2S are highly toxic and corrosive. Additionally, H2S plays crucial roles in both human physiological and pathological processes. It has been recognized as the third physiological gas transmitter, alongside CO and NO [4,5]. Healthy human blood typically contains hydrogen sulfide levels ranging from 10 to 100 µM [6]. Research indicates a close correlation between hydrogen sulfide activity and diseases such as Alzheimer's, diabetes, Down syndrome, and cancer [7–10]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop highly sensitive and selective techniques for detecting sulfides in both environmental and physiological contexts. Fluorescence methods have emerged as the preferred detection techniques, surpassing alternatives like electrochemical methods, chromatography, and colorimetry. Their advantage lies in their speed, high sensitivity, and capability for on-site detection. Currently, numerous single-emission fluorescent sensors utilizing the sulfide-metal displacement principle have been created [11–13]. Despite their specificity, these probes often require complex synthesis procedures and lengthy preparation times. Furthermore, the random movement of organic molecules can lead to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [14]. To address these challenges, Liu et al. synthesized a RhB/Uio-66-N3 ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on azide for intracellular sulfur ions detection and imaging [15]. Zhao et al. developed S′LnMOF, which features dual emission bands. They used vinyl as a functionalized ligand in conjunction with Eu3+ ions to enable the quantitative analysis of sulfur ions in water, employing smartphone-based detection techniques [16]. Zou et al. integrated copper clusters with carbon dots to create CuNCs/CNQDs proportional fluorescent probe tailored for detecting hydrogen sulfide in carrion samples [17]. Clearly, ratiometric fluorescence has significant advantages over single-emission signals as it can mitigate the effects of external factors, enhance sensitivity and reproducibility, and visually discriminate colors through multiple emission peak alterations. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop an efficient and intuitive proportional fluorescence strategy for on-site sulfide detection.

In recent years, there has been a transition in the carriers of fluorescent sensors from liquid phase to solid phase, involving test strips and hydrogels. In addition, analytical equipment has transitioned from large fluorescence spectrometers to more accessible smartphones. The integration of fluorescent composite materials with smartphones and artificial intelligence holds great significance for the development of fast, convenient, and accurate sensors. Machine learning (mL), as a branch of artificial intelligence, has been widely used in materials [18], environment [19], biology [20], clinical medicine [21] and other fields. In comparison to smartphones, mL has the capability to extract a greater number of signals from images for analysis [22]. Moreover, its robust data analysis and fitting capabilities enable variables with consistent patterns to undergo multivariate regression. Using the 'training set' and 'test set' for verification allows for further confirmation of the accuracy of the fitting.

Studies have reported the ability of paramagnetic Cu2+ to form stable metal complexes with nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds. Cu2+ effectively quenches the fluorescence of the probe through electron or energy transfer mechanisms [23,24]. Our focus is on protoporphyrin IX (Ppix), which contains a pyrrole ring. We assume it forms a stable metal coordination complex with Cu2+. On the other hand, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have important potential in fluorescence sensing applications due to their porosity, photophysical properties and guest encapsulation capabilities. The incorporation of MOFs into organic molecules serves to reduce aggregation and enhance fluorescence stability [14]. This approach offers a promising method for developing high-performance sulfide sensors, leveraging the advantages of both MOFs and organic probes.

In this study, Uio-66-NH2/Ppix exhibits two distinct peaks upon excitation at 365 nm. The red fluorescence of Ppix undergoes quenching upon the addition of Cu2+ and enhancement upon subsequent addition of S2-, leading to an On-Off-On phenomenon ( Scheme 1A). Simultaneously, the blue fluorescence emitted by Uio-66-NH2 remains consistently stable, serving as a self-calibration mechanism that effectively mitigates background interference. The proposed method demonstrates a superior detection limit and response time in comparison to other studies (Table. S1). Significantly, a multivariate linear regression model has been meticulously crafted to precisely predict S2- by leveraging light excitation imaging, extracting image fingerprint information through Python programming, and applying machine learning algorithms for comprehensive data analysis (Scheme 1B). The proposed multifunctional sensor, based on machine learning, exhibits significant potential for the real-time detection of S2-.
The reagents and characterization instruments utilized in the experiments are provided in the Supplementary Information (SI) Text S1.

Uio-66-NH2 was synthesized with slight modifications following the procedure outlined in the literature [25]. Specifically, zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 0.234 g, 1 mmol) was added to 35 mL of N,N′-dimethylformamide (DMF), and then 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2, 0.182 g, 1 mmol) was introduced. After 5 min of sonication, the mixture underwent a solvothermal reaction at 120 °C for 24 h. The resulting brownish-yellow crystals were collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 8 min), washed with DMF and ethanol to remove any residual reactants. Finally, the Uio-66-NH2 powder was dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight. The obtained Uio-66-NH2 was dissolved in PBS buffer (pH=7) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The probe solution (Uio-66-NH2/Ppix) was prepared by mixing equal volumes of Ppix (200 μM, dissolved in DMF) and Uio-66-NH2. 100 μL of the probe solution was added to 1.8 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 50 μL of Cu2+ and an incubation period of 5 min. Subsequently, S2- of varying concentrations (50 μL) were added and incubated for an additional 5 min. The emission spectra ranging from 400 nm to 700 nm was measured using a fluorometer with the following settings: excitation wavelength of 365 nm, scanning rate of 1200 nm/min, photomultiplier negative high voltage of 600 V, and EX and EM slit widths of 5 mm. Cu2+ and S2- concentrations were quantified by determining the fluorescence intensity ratio (F629/F431) at respective wavelengths of 431 nm and 629 nm. In selectivity experiments, the same procedures were followed, except that S2- was substituted with other interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging and feature data extraction
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, fluorescent solution samples were prepared with five different concentration gradients: 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 µM. Each concentration level's solution was replicated ten times, resulting in a total of fifty fluorescent images. Subsequently, OpenCV software was employed to extract color features from these images. The average color values of different color channels RGB (Red, Green, Blue) in the images were calculated and utilized as the visual fingerprint information for each sample. These extracted features were combined with corresponding concentration information and class labels to construct a training dataset. The concentration levels were categorized as A, B, C, D, and E, with appropriate labels assigned to each sample. To better comprehend the distribution of the data, violin plots of the original data were generated.

Regarding model building, the Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) model was established using the MASS package in the R programming language. With the assistance of the caret package, the constructed training dataset was randomly stratified into training and testing sets. The LDA model underwent a five-fold cross-validation process. The training process of the LDA model involved projecting data from the three-dimensional RGB space to a two-dimensional space for classification purposes. The projection vectors obtained from training were used to project the test dataset, and predictions were made based on the projected results in conjunction with pre-defined decision thresholds.

Uio-66-NH2 exhibits good dispersity as observed under SEM and TEM with an average particle size of ∼107 nm, displaying a regular octahedral crystal structure ( Fig. 1a-c). Mapping analysis demonstrates a uniform distribution of C, N, O, and Zr elements on the product's surface. (Fig. S1). EDS and XPS spectra provide quantitative analysis of the weight percentages of the four elements: C (38.44 wt%), N (4.79 wt%), O (23.15 wt%), and Zr (33.63 wt%) (Fig. 1d-e). High-resolution XPS analysis provides information on the chemical bonds of Uio-66-NH2. Zr 3d shows spin-split peaks at Zr 3d5/2 (182.7 eV) and Zr 3d3/2 (185.1 eV), consistent with previous literature [26] (Fig. 1f). Furthermore, the C 1 s spectrum displays three groups of peaks in Fig. S2: C-C/CC (284.6 eV), C-O/C-N (285.7 eV), and CO (288.6 eV) [27]. The O1s spectrum reveals three chemical bonds: O-Zr (530.7 eV), OC (531.7 eV), and O-C (533.1 eV) [28]. The N1s spectrum exhibits peaks at -NH2 (398.9 eV) and N-C (399.6 eV), along with a peak at 400.2 eV corresponding to the interaction between protons and amino groups forming -NH3+ [29,30]. XRD pattern displays diffraction peaks at 7.28°, 8.42°, and 25.65° (Fig. 1g), which can be attributed to the (111), (200), and (600) crystal planes of Uio-66-NH2, respectively. The sharp and intense peak shapes indicate the high crystallinity of the product, consistent with simulated spectra [31,32], confirming the successful synthesis. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1h) shows peaks at 3368 cm−1, 3435 cm−1, and 1622 cm−1, attributed to amino vibrations on the aromatic ring. The peak at 1653 cm−1 indicates the presence of residual DMF within the framework [33]. Peaks observed at 1571 cm−1, 1433 cm−1, and 1387 cm−1 correspond to O-CO bonds, characteristic of the BDC-NH2 raw material. This suggests that the UiO-66-NH2 is synthesized via a synergistic interaction between Zr4+ and carboxyl groups, endowing it with a stable structure [34]. Moreover, the peak at 1257 cm−1 is associated with the C-N vibration of the aromatic amine, and peaks at 667 cm−1 and 770 cm−1 are indicative of Zr-O bonds [35]. These findings align with the XPS analysis.

Fig. 1i illustrates the optical properties of UiO-66-NH2, including distinctive absorption bands at 250 nm and 323 nm, corresponding to π-π * transitions within the aromatic ring and n-π * transitions associated with the amino group [36]. UiO-66-NH2 exhibits vibrant azure fluorescence at 428 nm when optimally excited at 337 nm, representing an embodiment of intra-ligand charge transfer [37]. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2 remains remarkably stable, unaffected by changes in the excitation wavelength (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism based on ratiometric fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Protoporphyrin IX (Ppix), a specific fluorophore used in this study, has a large Stokes shift of approximately 227 nm (Fig. S4). The blue fluorescence of Uio-66-NH2 serves as the reference signal, forming a ratiometric system with Uio-66-NH2/Ppix ( Fig. 2a). Pure Ppix exhibits fluorescence quenching in water due to its poor water solubility and self-aggregation (Fig. 2a, image b). However, the addition of Uio-66-NH2 greatly enhances the intensity of red fluorescence at 629 nm, while blue fluorescence is emitted at 428 nm (Fig. 2a, curve c). This phenomenon is attributed to the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The emission spectra of Uio-66-NH2 overlaps obviously with the absorption spectra of Ppix (Fig. 2b). Uio-66-NH2 serves as the donor fluorophore and as an antenna for absorbing photons, and energy is transferred from the Uio-66-NH2 donor to the Ppix receptor. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, as the Ppix concentration rises, the fluorescence intensity of Uio-66-NH2 steadily declines, whereas the fluorescence intensity of Ppix increases. Previous reports have shown that Cu2+ can quench the blue fluorescence of pure Uio-66-NH2 [38], consistent with our experimental results (Fig. S5). Fortunately, the addition of Cu2+ in Uio-66-NH2/Ppix preferentially interacts with Ppix in the system, leading to the quenching of red fluorescence (Fig. S6), at which time the intensity of the blue fluorescence increases slightly due to the cut-off of the energy transfer pathway (Fig. 2a, curve d). The red fluorescence quenching may be attributed to the heavy metal effect on phosphorescent emission [39]. Subsequently, upon addition of excess S2- to Uio-66-NH2/Ppix/Cu2+, the fluorescence at 629 nm is immediately restored and even enhanced by approximately 100-fold. Significantly, the Uio-66-NH2/Ppix system demonstrates a substantial peak shift of 198 nm between both emission peaks. This shift effectively eliminates spectral overlap, enabling distinct color changes and thereby enhancing visual sensitivity. As depicted in Fig. 2d, the coordinate of Uio-66-NH2/Ppix on the standard colorimetric system (CIE) diagram (point c) is (0.257, 0.115), but it shifts to blue upon addition of Cu2+ (point d). Following the subsequent addition of S2-, a more substantial color shift is observed, with the coordinate changing from (0.164, 0.084) to (0.338, 0.147). This pronounced shift results in a notable alteration in the solution's color, which significantly aids in the visual evaluation of S2-.

In the ultraviolet-visible region, Ppix displays several absorption peaks owing to its extensive π-conjugated system. These peaks are attributed to the π-π * transitions within the porphyrin ring (Fig. 2e). The Soret band is characterized by two prominent peaks at 350 nm and 449 nm, while the Q band includes comparatively weaker absorption peaks at 534 nm, 590 nm, and 642 nm. However, upon the introduction of Uio-66-NH2 to Ppix, the two peaks corresponding to the Soret band vanish, and a new peak emerges at 380 nm. Notably, a noticeable change in the color of Ppix in water is observable (Fig. 2e, image b, c), suggesting an interaction between the two. This interaction is likely attributed to coupling effects, supported by the appearance of two new infrared vibrational peaks at 1704 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1 assigned to amide bonds (Fig. S7) [36]. It is hypothesized that the introduction of Uio-66-NH2 restricts the molecular motion of Ppix, preventing its improper aggregation and free molecular motion. Consequently, non-radiative decay possibilities decrease, and pathways for energy transfer open up, ultimately resulting in enhanced fluorescence at 629 nm [40]. Upon adding Cu2+ to Uio-66-NH2/Ppix, the intensity of the new peak decreases, and three peaks in the Q band are reduced and weakened, indicating the formation of copper-porphyrin complexes. Compared to free porphyrin, metalloporphyrin complexes have more symmetric structures and reduced splitting of orbital energy levels, resulting in degeneracy in Q-band peaks (Fig. 2e, curve d). The 1H NMR spectra also shows that Cu2+ coordinates with the pyrrole nitrogen inside the porphyrin ring, as well as the disappearance of the pyrrole nitrogen's proton signal at − 3.54 ppm in the high field (Fig. S8). In addition, it’s hypothesized that the copper-protoporphyrin complex slightly altered the chemical environment of Uio-66-NH2, thereby causing a 3 nm red shift in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2a). The characteristic peaks of porphyrin re-emerge completely with further addition of S2-, indicating the displacement of Cu2+ from porphyrins (Fig. 2e, curve e). To confirm whether S2- displace Cu2+ from porphyrins, pure CuS is prepared as a control substance (Fig. 2f). Comparing with the XRD spectra of Uio-66-NH2, Uio-66-NH2/Ppix/Cu2+/S2- shows new diffraction peaks at 29.3°, 31.8°, and 47.9°, corresponding to the (102), (103), and (110) crystal planes of pure CuS [41], confirming the formation of CuS as a significant factor contributing to the restoration of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tandem detection of Cu2+ and S2- based on fluorometer
ce:section.ce:para: Before proceeding with linear detection experiments, various aspects of the probe's performance were investigated, including optimal pH response, temperature effects, and reaction kinetics. A detailed description can be found in Supplementary Text S2, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. S9 and Fig. S10. As depicted in Fig. 3a, the addition of Cu2+ transforms the probe solution from red to blue. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F629/F431) exhibits distinct nonlinear and linear correlations with the concentration of Cu2+ within the ranges of 0.1–10 μM and 0.1–2 μM, respectively (Fig. 3b). The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ is calculated as 55 nM using the formula LOD= 3σ/S (referenced in SI). This LOD is significantly below the maximum permissible concentration for Cu2+ in potable water set by the U.S Environmental Protection Agency (20 μM) [41]. To assess the extent of quenching of porphyrin by Cu2+, the Stern-Volmer equation is employed to determine a quenching constant of KSV = 6.2 × 105 M−1 (Fig. S11a). This constant highlights the effectiveness of Cu2+ as a quencher for Ppix [39]. Within the Uio-66-NH2/Ppix system, the fluorescence of Ppix is almost entirely quenched by an equimolar amount of Cu2+ (5 μM) (Fig. 3b), so succeeding experiments utilize a Cu2+ quenching concentration of 5 μM to achieve optimal sensitivity. Additionally, Fig. S12 illustrates the excellent selectivity and interference resilience of Uio-66-NH2/Ppix towards Cu2+, which may be attributed to the robust interaction between Cu2+ and porphyrin (binding constant Ka = 2.9 ×105 M−1) (Fig. S11b).

Considering the strong affinity of S2- for Cu2+ (Ksp (CuS) = 1.27 × 10−36 [42]), fluorescence titration experiments of Uio-66-NH2/Ppix/Cu2+ with S2- were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3c, the intensity of blue fluorescence at 431 nm decreases by approximately 27% then stabilizes in the presence of S2-. Meanwhile, the fluorescence at 629 nm exhibits a continuous increase corresponding to the rising concentration of S2-. There exists an excellent linear correlation between F629/F431 values and S2- concentration within the range of 10–90 μM, with a fitted equation of F629/F431 = 0.028 * [S2-]− 0.252 (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 3d). SI supports the calculation of a low LOD of 35 nM, which is significantly below the World Health Organization's specified limit for sulfides in freshwater (∼15 μM) [43]. In contrast, S2- enhances the fluorescence of Uio-66-NH2/Ppix at 629 nm due to alkaline effect in the absence of Cu2+, with a calculated LOD of 1.03 μM (Fig. S13). Interestingly, the probe solution was found to be reversible, further confirming the displacement mechanism between Cu-Ppix complex and the target analyte S2- (Fig. S14).

In order to investigate the practicality of the sensor in complex environments, a series of common amino acids and anions were employed. As shown in Figs. 3e and S15, significant increase in fluorescence emission at 629 nm, approximately 100-fold, specifically in the presence of S2-. Even when coexisting with these substances (Fig. 3f), the F629/F431 ratio remain consistent, even at concentrations five times higher than that of S2-. These results showcase the remarkable specificity of Uio-66-NH2/Ppix/Cu2+ towards S2- and its strong interference resistance. To assess the suitability of the probe in real environments. river water and lake water samples were collected from Maoming, China. The results are summarized in Table. S2, showing recoveries of S2- ranging from 96.77% to 105.50%, with relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) within the range 2.50–5.30%. These findings highlight the probe's accuracy and precision in detecting S2- in actual water samples. Further, in order to explore whether the probe can be used to detect gaseous H2S in the air, the gas mixture containing H2S (1% in N2) was drummed into the probe solution. The response signal was still discernible, and the color of the solution was lavender under ultraviolet light (Fig. S16).

After a comprehensive analysis of the probe's detection capabilities, we identified a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the probe and the concentration of S2- within a specific concentration range. This relationship highlights the probe's efficacy for fluorescence-based visualization of S2- detection, as illustrated in Fig. S17. Image feature information was extracted from fluorescence visualization photographs at concentrations of 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 µM, corresponding to concentration categories A to E. This dataset, inclusive of image features and their associated concentration and category labels, constituted the training dataset (Table S3, left). To gain a deeper understanding of the data distribution, a violin plot of the original data was generated ( Fig. 4a). The plot visually conveys the compact nature of the Blue and Green feature data distributions, while the Red feature data exhibits a broader range of variability, which emphasizes the notable responsiveness of red fluorescence in this study. To ensure uniformity in data scale and range, the RGB image features were standardized (Table S3, right). Three-dimensional scatter plots showcase the spatial distribution of samples pre- and post-standardization (Fig. S18), indicating that samples with similar concentrations cluster consistently, even after standardization. Subsequently, a thorough statistical analysis was performed to determine the mean (Average, avg), standard deviation (Sd), standard error (Se), and confidence interval (Ci) of various concentration samples for the three image features, both pre- and post-standardization (Table S4). Radial bar charts, derived from these statistics, visually represent feature variations in relation to concentration. Notably, the Blue and Green features remain relatively consistent across concentrations, while the Red feature displays a pronounced increase with concentration (Fig. 4b). Additionally, the post-standardization data ranges approximately between − 2 and 1 (Fig. 4c).

Utilizing the MASS package in R programming language, a Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) model was developed and subjected to a five-fold cross-validation. Two-dimensional classification plots, derived from training outcomes, showcase the model's capability to differentiate samples based on S2- concentrations ( Fig. 5a). Following dimensionality reduction, LD1 and LD2 demonstrate accurate category recognition, collectively accounting for over 95% of the variance. Additionally, confusion matrices (Fig. 5b) and accuracy bar charts (Fig. 5c) are employed to objectively assess model performance from training and prediction outcomes. These visuals conclusively verify the accurate classification of samples across both training and testing sets, attaining a precision rate of 1. This highlights the robustness of the LDA model in characterizing fluorescence responses to varied S2- concentrations.

Further, the quantitative association between RGB feature values and concentration was systematically investigated. This association is visualized through a correlation heatmap ( Fig. 6a), complemented by multivariate statistical methods (Fig. S19). Notably, the Blue and Green features show minimal correlations with concentration (RBlue = 0.001, RGreen = 0.33), in contrast to the Red feature which exhibits a strong positive correlation (RRed = 0.99). Then, the dataset was meticulously divided into training and testing sets, and an extensive regression analysis was performed using the RGB features as dependent variables. As shown in Fig. S19, statistical analyses reveal that the Blue and Green feature responses are not statistically significant (p > 0.05), while the Red feature displays a pronounced correlation with concentration (p < 0.01). The model was subsequently optimized, focusing on a specialized linear regression analysis between the Red feature and concentration, yielding the equation CONC. = 0.5238 Red + - 17.8106. Utilizing this model, we accurately predicted concentrations for the testing set. The tight congruence between the regression line and predicted concentrations (Fig. 6b) highlights the reliability of the model. These results emphasize the efficacy of red fluorescence data as an S2- concentration indicator, holding significant potential for pollution detection endeavors.

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorescent probe, Uio-66-NH2/Ppix, exhibiting simplicity in preparation and efficacy, was developed based on the sulfide-metal substitution mechanism to sequentially detect detecting copper and sulfide ions in aqueous environments. The fluorescence detection of sulfide reveals an extensive linear range (10–90 μM) and a low LOD of 35 nM, highlighting advantages such as swift response, high sensitivity, remarkable selectivity, and reversibility. Furthermore, employing machine learning algorithms to decipher color signals from fluorescent images has enhanced the precision of online sulfide analysis. This research introduces a novel and unique fluorescent detection methodology, providing invaluable insights into not only sulfide detection but also the analysis of diverse pollutants in aquatic environments.

The developed intelligent probe using UiO-66-NH2/Ppix MOF holds significant environmental implications. While the studied material may not be inherently hazardous, the intelligent detection of sulfur ions addresses environmental concerns. Sulfide ions often stem from industrial and natural sources, leading to water pollution and ecosystem disruption. This work offers a sensitive and rapid detection solution, aiding in early pollution detection and remediation efforts. By applying machine learning for accurate quantification, the probe contributes to efficient monitoring of sulfide ions, thereby safeguarding water quality and ecosystem health. This technology underscores its potential for broader pollution detection, aligning with environmental protection goals.

Degui Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Long Yu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Xin Li: Data curation. Yunfei Lu: Investigation. Chaoqun Niu: Validation. Penghui Fan: Validation. Houjuan Zhu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Bing Chen: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Suhua Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U21A20290, 82001957, 22176044), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515011656), the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT (2023rcyj1003), and Maoming Science and Technology Project (2023382).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2023.132950.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A

.
